title: Red Sea vs Arabian Gulf: Comparing the Fixing Activity Behind the Barrels
section_path: Red Sea vs Arabian Gulf: Comparing the Fixing Activity Behind the Barrels
section_path_text: Red Sea vs Arabian Gulf: Comparing the Fixing Activity Behind the Barrels
summary:
Main
Over the spring of 2026, we logged 354 reported tanker fixtures across two of the Middle East's principal crude gateways and set them side by side to compare how each region's fixing activity behaves: Yanbu on Saudi Arabia's Red Sea coast and the wider Arabian Gulf (AG) complex stretching from Fujairah and the UAE to Oman. The aim is to look past the raw fixture count to a more useful question: how the two regions' fixture volumes compare by barrel, by ship size, and by terminal, and to do so against the backdrop of an unusually disrupted quarter. Three observations emerge from the data.

children.title: Main
children.summary: Over the spring of 2026, we logged 354 reported tanker fixtures across two of the Middle East's principal crude gateways and set them side by side to compare how each region's fixing activity behaves: Yanbu on Saudi Arabia's Red Sea coast and the wider Arabian Gulf (AG) complex stretching from Fujairah and the UAE to Oman. The aim is to look past the raw fixture count to a more useful question: how the two regions' fixture volumes compare by barrel, by ship size, and by terminal, and to do so against the backdrop of an unusually disrupted quarter.

children.title: Activity vs. Volume - More Ships in the Gulf, More Barrels from Yanbu
section_path: Activity vs. Volume - More Ships in the Gulf, More Barrels from Yanbu
children.section_path_text: Activity vs. Volume - More Ships in the Gulf, More Barrels from Yanbu
children.summary: At first glance, the Gulf looks busier, and on a pure fixture count, it is. But counting ships flatters the picture.

children.title: The Dirty Book - Where the Two Regions Genuinely Diverge
section_path: The Dirty Book - Where the Two Regions Genuinely Diverge
children.section_path_text: The Dirty Book - Where the Two Regions Genuinely Diverge
children.summary: The differences become clearer when focusing on dirty cargoes - crude oil and fuel oil - and examining fixture activity by vessel class. The divergence is concentrated in the VLCC segment.

children.title: Gulf Load Ports - Fujairah Leads, Ras Tanura Goes Quiet
section_path: Gulf Load Ports - Fujairah Leads, Ras Tanura Goes Quiet
children.section_path_text: Gulf Load Ports - Fujairah Leads, Ras Tanura Goes Quiet
children.summary: The Gulf's loadings are spread across more than a dozen named terminals, but the spot market clearly runs through Fujairah, which alone accounts for 76 fixtures, ahead of Sohar (32), Duqm (29), and Mina Al Fahal (21). What draws the eye, though, is at the bottom of the graph.

children.title: Market Context - A Quarter Shaped by an Unresolved Crisis
section_path: Market Context - A Quarter Shaped by an Unresolved Crisis
children.section_path_text: Market Context - A Quarter Shaped by an Unresolved Crisis
children.summary: None of the above patterns should be viewed in isolation. The fixture window coincided with a period of regional disruption, making the data a reflection of market behaviour under exceptional conditions rather than a structural shift in Middle East crude trade.

children.title: The Takeaway
children.summary: On fixture count alone, the Arabian Gulf and Yanbu appear broadly comparable. A closer examination of cargo volumes, vessel deployment, and loading locations, however, reveals distinct market profiles.
